humanities_role_explanation: Utilizes a century-long historical archive of U.S. Treasury Bulletins (1939-2020s) as its corpus, treating these historical documents as raw quantitative data for financial retrieval, factual QA, and mathematical calculation rather than as objects of qualitative interpretive inquiry.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluates agents on text (both unstructured prose and structured tabular data) and images (visual interpretation of historical figures, charts, and scanned PDF pages).
analytical_tasks_explanation: Requires multi-step reasoning across unstructured text and complex nested tables, including longitudinal financial analysis (e.g., ordinary least squares linear regression), inflation adjustments, temporal revision verification, and high-precision arithmetic computations.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed as an enterprise grounded reasoning benchmark focused on historical financial document QA.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates frontier LLMs and agent frameworks including Claude Opus (4.5, 4.6), Claude Sonnet (4.5, 4.6), Claude Haiku 4.5, GPT-5 (5.1, 5.4, Mini), and Gemini 3 (Pro, 3.1 Pro, Flash).

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLMs and LLM-based agents are the primary systems being evaluated and benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes History, as it utilizes a 100-year historical archive of U.S. Treasury Bulletins spanning from 1939 onwards.

quantitative_metrics.note: Accuracy evaluated under varying absolute relative error thresholds (0.0%, 0.1%, 1.0%, 5.0%), exact match, and fuzzy text matching.

english_language.note: The paper, corpus, and questions are in English.

summary: OfficeQA Pro is an enterprise benchmark designed to evaluate AI agents on end-to-end grounded reasoning over a heterogeneous 100-year archive of U.S. Treasury Bulletins. It contains 133 highly complex and verifiable questions requiring precise document parsing, multi-document retrieval, and quantitative mathematical modeling to measure SOTA model limitations.
key_findings: Frontier agents struggle on over half of the questions, with the strongest agent (Claude Opus 4.6) scoring 48.1% when processing raw PDFs. Converting the PDFs to a structured representation yields a 16.1% relative accuracy gain on average and significantly decreases latency and cost. Surprisingly, AI agents consistently outperform human annotators in both speed and accuracy on a subset evaluation.
review_notes: Minor disagreements in 'analytical_tasks' were resolved by merging the lists to encompass the specific tasks mentioned in the paper. For 'primary_evaluation_setting', Ciop wrote 'agentic tool-use' while Cip wrote 'zero-shot'; these were combined into 'zero-shot agentic tool-use' to reflect that the models are evaluated in an autonomous agent workflow on a zero-shot basis.
